af_score_components.notes: Fullstack MCP framework with TypeScript and Python SDKs, React widget support, and optional Manufact cloud hosting. Solid MCP foundation with inspector tooling. Error handling is framework-level and left to implementers. Auth only applies to Manufact cloud deployment (GitHub OAuth); local/self-hosted has no auth. Rate limiting not applicable to a framework.

security_score_components.security_notes: Manufact cloud enforces HTTPS; local development may not need TLS. Cloud deployment requires GitHub OAuth for auth strength. Scope granularity is framework-level and depends on what each built server implements. Standard dependency hygiene expected for TS/Python SDKs. Secret handling follows env var patterns for cloud API keys.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Manufact cloud has implied SLA; self-hosted OSS has no SLA. Framework versioned releases expected; newer project so some churn possible. Recovery behavior for built servers depends on implementation patterns provided by the framework.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server bridging AI models to Windows WinDbg/CDB for crash dump analysis. Good MCP integration for a specialized debugging domain. Error handling is limited — crash dump analysis errors can be opaque and complex WinDbg commands may time out at 30s default. No auth required (local tool). No rate limits applicable.

security_score_components.security_notes: Local tool with no TLS requirement. No authentication — runs on developer's local Windows machine with no access controls. No scope granularity; tool exposes full WinDbg access once connected. Python package with limited dependencies, reasonable hygiene. Symbol server path via _NT_SYMBOL_PATH env var is standard practice. Enterprise policy review recommended before using with production crash dumps.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Local dev tool with no uptime SLA. Reasonable stability for a single-maintainer tool. Newer project with limited track record. Session state (open dump/remote connection) must be managed explicitly; forgetting to close sessions may leak resources. Command timeout default of 30s can cause reliability issues for complex analyses.

af_score_components.notes: Kubernetes-native MCP management platform with web dashboard, service marketplace, and Helm chart deployment. Solid platform concept but documentation is primarily Chinese with English lagging behind. gRPC internal transport makes protocol conversion errors opaque. Auth requires K8s + Helm setup with username/password plus built-in permission layer. Early-stage Kymo-MCP org project.

security_score_components.security_notes: Kubernetes deployment implies HTTPS is possible and expected via ingress. Built-in MCPCan-Authz permission management is a positive; however, default admin/admin123 credentials are a critical security risk if not changed post-install. Group-based access control provides reasonable scope granularity. Go/Vue stack with newer project; CVE hygiene unknown. Default credential risk is the primary concern.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: No SLA documented; early-stage project with unknown long-term maintenance commitment. Kubernetes infrastructure provides some inherent resilience. Limited track record for breaking changes history. gRPC errors may be opaque during service failures. Project stability questionable given Kymo-MCP org newness.

af_score_components.notes: Unified MCP aggregator with single HTTP/SSE endpoint, AI-powered semantic tool discovery via vector search, OAuth 2.0, and hot-swappable config. Core purpose is MCP aggregation — strong fit. Good documentation covering routing modes and auth. Downstream server failures propagate (no circuit-breaker documented). OAuth 2.0 + social login adds complexity. Default admin/admin123 credentials must be changed.

security_score_components.security_notes: Self-hosted but Docker deployment typically adds HTTPS via reverse proxy. Good auth options: OAuth 2.0, JWT/bcrypt, bearer token, social login (GitHub/Google). Group-based access control for per-server routing provides real scope granularity. Active Node/React project with reasonable dependency hygiene. Environment-based config for secrets. Default admin/admin123 is a deployment risk.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted OSS with no formal SLA. Active project with multiple releases and reasonable stability. OSS changelog available. Hot-swap config changes may cause brief tool unavailability during reload. Downstream server failures propagate to aggregated endpoint with no documented circuit-breaking or fallback. Tool name collision handling across servers not clearly documented.

af_score_components.notes: IMPORTANT: mcphub-nvim is a Neovim editor plugin that acts as an MCP CLIENT — it helps human developers connect to MCP servers from within Neovim. It is NOT an MCP server that agents can call; mcp_server_url is null and the package requires an active Neovim session. mcp_score=0. Documentation is good for a Neovim plugin. Error handling surfaces in editor buffers. Auth (OAuth PKCE + API key) is for downstream MCP servers being connected. Rate limiting depends on connected servers.

security_score_components.security_notes: Supports OAuth 2.0 with PKCE for remote MCP servers and API key auth — solid security model for a plugin. Scope control depends on connected server capabilities. Lua plugin with Node.js hub daemon; active development with reasonable hygiene. Environment variable expansion for credentials in config is the correct pattern. Credentials not hardcoded in documentation examples.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Local Neovim plugin with no SLA; uptime depends entirely on editor session. Versioned Neovim plugin with reasonable stability. Active development means some churn possible with breaking changes. Multi-instance scenarios require all Neovim instances to share the same hub daemon, adding coordination complexity. Project-local configs must be manually set up per workspace.

af_score_components.notes: Self-hosted MCP gateway aggregating multiple servers behind a single endpoint with canonical tool naming (server__tool format). Good MCP fit as a gateway. Documentation covers canonical naming and deployment modes. Upstream failures propagate; no circuit-breaker documented. Bearer token auth is simple. No rate limiting built in. Stateless vs stateful session mode requires explicit configuration.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS depends on deployment configuration; Docker deployment can be placed behind HTTPS proxy. Bearer token auth only — no user auth layer for the gateway itself; all-or-nothing access. No RBAC or fine-grained scope control. Go binary with minimal dependency surface typical for Go projects. Custom headers for downstream auth credentials passed through via config.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted OSS with no SLA. Stable Go binary releases. Newer project with some track record. Stateless vs stateful mode trade-offs require careful configuration tuning. stdio servers require Docker image with Node.js/Python variant for subprocess support, adding operational complexity.

af_score_components.notes: IMPORTANT: MCPO is an MCP-to-OpenAPI proxy — it converts MCP servers to OpenAPI/REST endpoints. It does NOT expose an MCP server interface itself (has_mcp_server=false). Agents call MCPO via standard HTTP/OpenAPI, not MCP protocol. mcp_score=0. Auto-generated /docs is a significant documentation benefit. Protocol translation may lose some MCP error context. API key auth is simple; OAuth 2.1 for upstream adds complexity. Rate limiting not documented.

security_score_components.security_notes: Self-hosted proxy; HTTPS enforcement depends on deployment. API key (--api-key) and OAuth 2.1 with dynamic client registration provide reasonable auth options. No fine-grained scope control at the proxy level. Python project from active open-webui org with reasonable dependency hygiene. API keys via CLI parameter or env var; OAuth 2.1 standard flows for upstream credentials.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted OSS with no SLA. Active open-webui org provides reasonable maintenance commitment. Translation layer may experience churn as MCP protocol evolves. Hot-reload of config file causes brief tool unavailability. No circuit-breaker or fallback for upstream MCP server failures documented.

af_score_components.notes: .NET library (NuGet) for building MCP servers and clients with attribute-based annotations. Enables .NET developers to expose existing business logic as MCP tools. Solid library for .NET ecosystem. Documentation requires XML doc comments for auto-generated tool descriptions. Error handling is library-level; consuming app handles actual error scenarios. No built-in auth (developer responsibility). Rate limiting outside library scope.

security_score_components.security_notes: No built-in TLS enforcement — consuming application must implement HTTPS transport. No built-in authentication layer; developer must implement entirely. No scope or permission control built in. NuGet package in .NET ecosystem with reasonable dependency hygiene. No secrets management built in; consuming app responsible for credential handling. Library approach means security is entirely the developer's responsibility.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Library has no uptime SLA — consuming application determines reliability. NuGet versioned with Semantic Kernel integration provides ecosystem stability. Newer project; some API churn possible. Classes from external libraries require explicit MCPServer.Register<T>() calls rather than auto-discovery, which could cause runtime issues if missed. Error recovery is application responsibility.

af_score_components.notes: IMPORTANT: MCPStore is a Python orchestration library wrapping MCP services for agent frameworks (LangChain, LangGraph, AutoGen, CrewAI, LlamaIndex). It is NOT itself an MCP server (has_mcp_server=false). Agents use MCPStore as a Python SDK to manage MCP service connections, not as a callable MCP endpoint. mcp_score=0. REST API for service management is unauthenticated by default. Error handling relies on underlying framework defaults. No rate limiting. Redis required for multi-process shared cache.

security_score_components.security_notes: REST API management endpoint is unauthenticated by default — any caller can manage MCP services. No built-in TLS enforcement. No scope control or RBAC. Redis dependency adds an attack surface. Python package with FastAPI; dependency hygiene is moderate. API headers for downstream MCP service auth are passed at service setup time via config, which is acceptable but no vault/rotation support documented.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Library/orchestration tool with no SLA. Newer project (pip installable) with limited track record. Early-stage; API churn expected. Redis dependency is a reliability requirement for multi-process deployments. The for_agent() isolation pattern requires developer discipline to prevent accidental context sharing. No documented retry or reconnection behavior for dropped MCP service connections.

af_score_components.notes: Go-based CLI for interacting with MCP servers — developer debugging and testing tool with mock server mode for client testing. Provides commands to list/call tools, interactive shell, web UI, and MCP config management across editors. Primary use case is developer tooling; mock server mode is for testing only and does not persist state. Excellent documentation for a CLI tool. Error output is clear and useful for debugging. No built-in auth; passes env vars and custom headers through to target servers. Rate limiting is downstream server concern.

security_score_components.security_notes: Local developer CLI with no TLS enforcement needed for stdio mode; HTTP mode connections should use HTTPS. No built-in auth — credentials passed through via env vars and custom HTTP headers to target MCP servers. No scope control; full access to all tools on target MCP server once connected. Go binary with minimal dependency surface (typical Go hygiene). Environment variable passthrough for credentials is the correct pattern.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Developer CLI tool with no uptime SLA applicable. Homebrew-installable Go binary with versioned releases indicates reasonable stability. Well-maintained for a developer tool. CLI exits cleanly on error; no persistent state in normal usage. Note: binary installs as 'mcptools' but aliasing as 'mcp' conflicts with some systems — docs recommend 'mcpt' alias. Mock server mode for testing only, no persistence.
